A memoir

Montage of Byurakan

Tigran
Byurakan, Armenia

These are the summers that stay. Not as memories exactly — as textures. The smell of chalk in cool corridors. The weight of a dried worm. The sound a wooden floor makes when you cross it toward sleep.

Tigran — Byurakan
I    The gazebo

Two brothers are working with mud in a puddle next to a small dirt road leading to a gazebo in the middle of a lush garden. There are raspberry bushes and roses on the side, and large fruit trees shading the path.

The gazebo — Byurakan

Kneeling down on a dry patch of dirt, the younger kid is carefully leading a worm to walk up the face of his palm from the mud — and smiles in anticipation of his catch. He immediately gets up and leaving the brother behind runs to the gazebo.

There is a young girl — tomboy looking — chatting with an older boy, sitting around an aluminium table scorching hot from the summer sun. The worm placed on the clean hot patch makes a small quiver and quick death; this alchemism turns the worm into a scorched stick.

It was a pride to them how the stick maintained its form without breaking.

The garden path — the elder brother

New Year — the brothers

II    The apricot tree

There was a large apricot tree to the left of the gazebo, in front of the two-storey apartment building made out of large blocks of stone where the kids used to live. The tree grew slanted from its roots and had a large bark area a metre below its branches. Tigran ran up the tree and held up the branches — he loved climbing trees and eating the fruit.

He loved the pear tree across the dirt road because it bore the most fruit and stood the tallest amongst the peach, apple, and other dense trees. He was aware of snakes and didn’t like going in there by himself. But with his grandfather he would help to collect the ripe fruits that fell into the large cotton cloth — some of them had burst, spreading their sweet aroma drenched in the heat of the summer sun.

The building — abandoned and decaying, the apricot tree long gone from the entrance

III    The apartment

Back in the apartment — more of a cabin, with an entrance in front by the apricot tree, and a beautiful patio in the back next to the kitchen. There were jars of fruit in the kitchen being saved for winter; they would eventually turn into the sweetest types of nectar. Jars of fresh milk would be bought once a week from a villager that walked around in the public side outside of the astronomy quarter gates.

You had to go through a corridor to enter the home, and below the stairway there was leftover plaster which looked like pieces of chalk — the whitest chalk you could find, and it was always a treat to take a piece. It had the smell of dark cool spaces and lifeless beauty.

The room felt welcoming and loved the company of human life.

The living room had two large windows overlooking the apricot tree and the garden behind it. Light would flow in and sunrays would illuminate parts of the wooden shelf on top of the old green couch. There was a sense of Kafka in the design of the place — a flare of the seventies in the furniture, but with great coolness in design and the scientific, cold arrangement of personal things on the shelves.

IV    The sleeping room

The master bedroom had a beautiful wooden floor that held the aroma of the years and made muted symphonies as you walked toward the corridor. It was made just as in the story of the three little bears — there was a large bed, a smaller bed next to it, and by the window toward the back was the cradle, with an overgrown child sleeping comfortably in it every night. The window by the cradle had a curtain that let only the warmest, softest light in.

The bathroom was on the left of the corridor. There were many games to play and adventures to do.

Epilogue

The same trees. The same light falling through them. He is still holding something small — still weighing it in his hands.

Byurakan  •  Armenia  •  Astronomy Quarter
